title: Cat Claws Growing Into Paw Pads: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do when your cat's claws grow into their paw pads. Understand the urgency, symptoms of infection, and how to get help.
content_html:
What Is an Ingrown Cat Claw?

When a cat is unable to naturally wear down their claws through scratching, the nails can grow excessively long and curve back into the paw pads. This is particularly common in senior cats or those with mobility issues. Unlike human nails, cat claws grow in layers; when these layers aren't shed, the nail thickens and forms a sharp hook that can eventually pierce the sensitive tissue of the paw, leading to pain, inflammation, and potential infection.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for an ingrown claw is Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a significant welfare concern because it is extremely painful for your cat. If you see blood, discharge, or if your cat is limping or obsessively licking the area, you should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours. Do not attempt to pull the nail out if it is deeply embedded, as this can cause further trauma or bleeding.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Sending a clear, well-lit photograph to your veterinarian or a triage service is incredibly helpful. It allows a professional to see the depth of the nail penetration and check for signs of secondary infection like swelling or pus. A photo helps the clinic determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and whether they need to prepare specific equipment for a minor surgical extraction or wound debridement.

faqs.question: Can I trim the ingrown nail at home?
faqs.answer: If the nail has already punctured the skin, it is highly recommended to seek professional help. Attempting to cut an embedded nail at home can cause intense pain and may result in the nail breaking off inside the wound.

faqs.question: Why do senior cats get ingrown nails more often?
faqs.answer: Older cats often develop thicker, more brittle nails and may suffer from arthritis, which makes them less likely to use scratching posts to shed their outer nail sheaths.

faqs.question: What are the signs of infection in the paw pad?
faqs.answer: Look for significant redness, heat radiating from the paw, a foul odor, or yellowish discharge (pus) around the site where the nail enters the skin.
